article_title: Role perceptions and attitudes toward discretion at a decentralized regulatory frontline: The case of organic inspectors

You are an expert public policy analyst reviewing sustainability research articles. **Instructions:** - Answer ALL questions only based on the provided Codebook and Article - Provide specific citations when requested - Keep justifications concise and evidence-based - For Yes/No questions, choose definitively based on evidence - For Yes-or-No or multiple choice problems, answer from the given options only (the options are in parentheses) - Format your entire response using the XML template below to ensure each answer stays inside its tag. It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. Carter* Department of Political Science, University of Utah, Salt Lake City, UT, USA Abstract The complexity of decentralized regulatory contexts can threaten program ﬁdelity, particularly if it results in divergence between program goals and the intents of regulatory inspectors. This paper investigates how inspectors negotiate the conﬂicting demands of a decentralized program by examining how they perceive their regulatory roles – the primary responsibilities that inspectors ascribe to their functions and the entities to which they feel responsible – and how these role orientations are related to inspec- tors’ attitudes toward the use of discretion. The study ﬁndings indicate that in the decentralized administration of United States organic food regulations, inspectors experience multiple, and sometimes conﬂicting, role orientations. The presence of multiple role orientations, however, does not seem to affect how inspectors approach their responsibilities. The combined strengths of quantitative and qualitative data are leveraged to offer explanations for the study ﬁndings and identify avenues for future research. Introduction As the operative frontline of regulatory activity, the work of inspectors is an essential element of any regulatory program. Regulatory administration is a complex social phenomenon (Nielsen 2006), requiring inspectors to “be alternately informative, cajoling, educating, or punitive as needed to produce the desired levels of cooperation and compliance” (May & Wood 2003, pg. 118). The discretion that inspectors exercise can support regulatory effectiveness – if through “responsive regulation” inspectors take into account contextual and motivational considerations that differ across regulatees (Tyler 1990; Ayres & Braithwaite 1992). At the same time, too much ﬂexibility in inspector activity can pose a threat to regulatory program ﬁdelity if responsive actions undermine compliance goals or are perceived to reﬂect regulatory capture (May 2003; Pautz 2009; Pautz & Rinfret 2013). Understanding the manners in which inspectors exercise discretion requires considering the various inﬂuences that they face, including signals embedded in the content of policy (May & Winter 2007), interactions with regulatees (Fineman 1998; Nielsen 2006, 2007), and inspectors’ own value orientations (Hedge et al. 1988). Contemporary regulatory program designs impose additional and potentially conﬂicting pressures with which inspectors must contend. Movements to replace hierarchical program structures with administrative decentralization reliant on “regulatory intermediaries” (Levi-Faur & Starobin 2014) can both multiply and dilute the objectives that are commu- nicated to frontline workers (Sandfort 2000; Buffat 2015). The increasing prevalence of nongovernmental organizations as regulatory administrators introduces organizational incentives that often diverge from program aims (Dias & Maynard-Moody 2007; Buffat 2015). Programs that treat program targets as public service customers can induce frontline workers to emphasize client desires over policy goals (Fountain 2001; Dubois 2010). The complexity of decentralized policy contexts can pose a threat to program ﬁdelity, particularly if the result is a divergence between program goals and the operative intents of frontline workers (Meyers et al. 2001). This paper examines how inspectors negotiate and internalize the potentially conﬂicting demands of a decentralized program context, and whether the responsibilities that inspectors prioritize shape their propensity to adhere more strictly or loosely to mandated regulatory directives. This is accomplished through two research objectives. The ﬁrst objective is to understand how inspectors perceive their regulatory roles – the primary responsibilities that inspectors Correspondence: David P. The second objective is to understand how these role orientations are related to inspectors’ discretionary attitudes, understood as the extent to which inspectors believe regulatory action should closely adhere to regulatory program rules (Hill 2013). Inspector role perceptions and discretionary attitudes are studied in the context of organic food regulation under the United States Department of Agriculture’s (USDA’s) National Organic Program (NOP). The NOP context is chosen for two reasons. First, the NOP is quasi-voluntary, as regulatees are only subject to regulatory oversight if they wish to market their products as “organic.” Regulatees pay for program participation, introducing a tension between program participants as regulatees versus regulatory service clients (Conglianese & Lazer 2003). Second, although the NOP is charged with establishing national standards by which organic food production is regulated in the US, it relies on independent governmental and nongovernmental certiﬁcation organizations to ensure certiﬁed organic operations’ adherence to NOP regulations. The decentralized nature of NOP delivery raises the potential for the incentives and objectives that are introduced by independent administrative organizations to inﬂuence the manners in which inspectors approach their regulatory responsibilities (Sandfort 2000; Dias & Maynard-Moody 2007). The study follows a mixed method research design, leveraging the combined strengths of quantitative and qualita- tive data to depict how organic inspectors understand their function (Creswell 1994). A survey quantiﬁes inspector role perceptions and attitudes toward discretion. Interviews provide more nuanced descriptions of how inspectors understand their responsibilities, balance conﬂicting incentives and constraints, and exercise judgment while performing their regulatory duties. The study ﬁndings provide only limited support for the general expectation that inspector role perceptions shape, and are thereby likely associated with, inspectors’ attitudes toward discretion. Drawing on implementation theory and contextual, social, and institutional characteristics of the NOP regulatory setting, the paper offers explanations for the ﬁndings and opportunities for future research. Inspector role perceptions and attitudes toward discretion Regulatory administration scholars have studied the work of inspectors from several angles. For example, May and Winter (1999, 2000; Winter & May 2001; see also May & Wood 2003, Pautz & Rinfret 2013) focus on conceptualizing and measuring inspectors’ monitoring and enforcement activity, ﬁnding that inspectors’ approaches can be usefully described by the degree of formalism and coercion that they exhibit. Other studies identify discrete inﬂuences on inspector behavior, such as policy designs (May 1993), political signaling (May & Winter 2007), and the values held and expressed by inspectors (Hedge et al. 1988). Similarly, Nielsen (2006, 2007) draws attention to the nature of inspector-regulatee interactions to explain inspectors’ behavior, and the inﬂuence of factors such as regulatee reputa- tion and power in shaping inspector practices. This study takes a slightly different approach by focusing on the role orientations that inspectors express and the relationship that inspector role perceptions have with their attitudes toward discretion. Following role theory, inspector roles are deﬁned as reﬂecting the primary responsibilities that inspectors ascribe to their functions and the individuals or entities to which they feel responsible (Biddle 1986; Selden et al. 1999). Roles, thus, reﬂect what Evans (2010, 2011) refers to as street-level bureaucrat “alliances” within an implementation structure. Drawing attention to how inspectors understand their roles moves beyond cataloging discrete inﬂuences on inspector behavior to document how inspectors internalize different inﬂuences within their professional setting (e.g. Hedge et al. 1988; Brehm & Gates 1999; May & Winter 2007). In doing so, it highlights the roles with which inspectors identify as possible mechanisms by which regulatory organizations, program contexts, and individual inspector values are translated into inspectors’ discretionary attitudes. Importantly, this study seeks to understand inspector role perceptions in a decentralized regulatory program, as such contexts introduce structural complexity that may further complicate inspector roles when compared with more traditional program contexts (Brodkin 2012). In traditional regulatory settings, inspector roles are assumed to be shaped by two dominant principals: the regulatory bureaucracies inspectors are accountable to and the individuals they are charged with regulating (e.g. Pautz, 2010). While inspectors are (by deﬁnition) regulatory actors, the extent to which they identify with regulatees can shift inspector orientations to align with regulatee concerns (Nielsen 2006, 2007; Evans 2010; Pautz & Rinfret 2013). Decentralized regulatory programs introduce another commitment by which inspectors’ roles might be deﬁned: in a context in which inspectors are responsible to independent organizations D. P. Carter Regulatory inspector role perceptions 2 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 3: charged with regulatory administration, inspectors are incentivized to internalize employer interests in addition to regulatory objectives (Hupe et al. 2015). This study therefore incorporates three possible role perceptions: inspectors as regulatory monitors, inspectors as service providers to regulatee operations, and inspectors as representatives of independent employer organizations. Rather than develop a single uniform understanding of their regulatory function, inspectors are likely to perceive these multiple role orientations simultaneously, resulting in a “web of obligations, relationships, and meanings” that inspectors regularly navigate (Musheno & Maynard-Moody 2015, p. 169). Sometimes inspector role responsibilities might converge, for example, when regulatory program goals and the objectives of independent administrative organi- zations align. At other times, inspectors might perceive themselves to be caught between divergent obligations, such as when regulatory program directives and regulatee concerns compete for favorable consequences stemming from inspector decisionmaking. In these instances, it is likely that an inspector will resolve conﬂicting role orientations by prioritizing certain obligations over others (Evans & Harris 2004). Regardless of the extent to which inspector role perceptions most often align or conﬂict with one another, or which obligations inspectors prioritize, a baseline expectation is that inspectors operating in a decentralized regulatory program will be faced with navigating potentially diverging role perceptions: Hypothesis 1: In a regulatory context characterized by administrative decentralization, inspectors will perceive multi- ple, sometimes conﬂicting, role orientations. Beyond documenting the role orientations that inspectors perceive in a decentralized regulatory setting, this paper examines the relationship between inspectors’ role perceptions and their discretionary attitudes. In this study, discretionary attitudes are assumed to reﬂect inspectors’ comfort or aversion to the application of individual judgment in the execution of program requirements. In other words, the extent to which inspectors perceive what Hupe et al. (2015, p. 17) call “discretion-as-used” to be an appropriate element of inspectors’ responsibilities. Role theory indicates that individual attitudes and behavior can be anticipated by the roles that individuals internalize (Biddle 1986). This paper tests the general expectation that inspectors’ discretionary attitudes are shaped by, and will therefore be associated with, the regulatory role orientations with which inspectors more closely align. More speciﬁc hypotheses are facilitated by considering the objectives and interests of the entities associated with each of the inspector roles incorporated in this study: inspectors as regulatory monitors, employee representatives, and service providers. First, the more inspectors prioritize their responsibilities to a regulatory program over their obligation to their employing organizations, or the regulatees that they interact with, the more likely they are to adhere to the “discretion- ary space” formally constructed by the regulatory framework in question (Buffat 2015). In emphasizing their regulatory obligations, such inspectors are likely to exhibit more “rule-bound” (May & Winter 2000), less accommodating (Pautz & Rinfret 2013) inspection practices. Whether it be the result of internalization of policy goals (Meyers et al. 2001), or a sense of public service duty, this close adherence to mandated regulatory directives is expected to be reﬂected in a relative aversion to the use of discretion: Hypothesis 2: The more closely an inspector aligns with a regulatory monitor role orientation, the less likely he or she is to exhibit a favorable attitude toward the use of discretion. Second, the more an inspector identiﬁes as a representative of an independent administrative organization, the more likely they are to attend to organizational goals over strict adherence to policy directives. The multiplicity of organizational objectives that contend with program requirements likely favor a more ﬂexible application of regulatory directives (Meyers et al. 2001) – unless organizational incentives are aligned with a strict adherence to program rules, for example, through performance parameters (Considine 2000; Brown et al. 2006). Perhaps most notably, in a program context where regulatees pay for program services, greater inspector discretion translates into a more accommodative regulatory approach through which administrative organizations might attract and retain regulatee clients (Conglianese & Lazer 2003). Inspectors that prioritize their obligations to their employer organiza- tions, thereby aligning with an employee or representative role orientation, are expected to favor the use of discretion: Regulatory inspector role perceptions D. P. Carter © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 Page 4: Hypothesis 3: The more closely an inspector aligns with an employee representative role orientation, the more likely he or she is to exhibit a favorable attitude toward the use of discretion. Finally, regulatory inspections are inherently social interactions, and the nature of the inspector-regulatee relation- ship can affect how inspectors carry out their responsibilities (May & Winter 1999; Nielsen 2006, 2007). Inspectors may favor cooperative interactions and even “partnerships” with regulatees over more adversarial relationships, particularly when trust and interdependence between inspectors and regulatees are high (Pautz & Rinfret 2013). Furthermore, the decisions that inspectors make can have signiﬁcant consequences for regulatees (Nielsen 2015). In the case of organic inspections, for example, a farmer’s access to the organic foods market, and the price premium that comes with it, depend on the results of annual inspection reports and the inspector reporting the decisions that comprise them. Inspectors that perceive themselves to be service providers to regulatory clients are assumed to be more likely to sympathize with regulatee interests and concerns, and are therefore more likely to be comfortable exercising judgment, and perhaps ﬂexibility, in the application of regulatory standards: Hypothesis 4: The more closely an inspector aligns with a service provider role orientation, the more likely he or she is to exhibit a favorable attitude toward the use of discretion. Through these hypotheses, this study examines the relationship between inspector role perceptions and attitudes toward discretion in the context of organic food regulation in the US. The extent to which these theoretical expectations are likely to generalize beyond this context is touched on in the conclusion. The context is described in the next section, followed by a presentation of the study’s mixed method design. Study context and research design This paper examines the role perceptions and attitudes toward discretion of inspectors charged with administering organic food regulations under the USDA’s NOP. As required by the 1990 Organic Foods Production Act, operations (such as farms) that market and sell their products as “organic” must hold organic certiﬁcation under the NOP and adhere to the national organic standards outlined in NOP regulations.1 As such, the NOP can be considered a “quasi-voluntary” program – operations actively seek and participate in the NOP. Operations can avoid participation requirements by refraining from using the term “organic” in their product marketing and labeling. The NOP’s decentralized structure is modeled loosely after International Organization for Standardization (ISO) guidelines for accreditation and certiﬁcation systems. The NOP codiﬁes uniform national organic standards through administrative rulemaking, and accredits independent governmental, nonproﬁt, and for-proﬁt organizations (certiﬁers) to certify operations according to the regulations. As of 2013, the NOP had accredited 48 US based certiﬁers: 17 private for-proﬁt organizations, 11 nonproﬁts, and 20 from the public sector.2 Public sector certiﬁers can be further broken down into three different forms: 17 fall under state government programs, such as the Colorado Department of Agriculture Organic Program; two fall under California county governments; and one operates out of Clemson University’s Department of Plant Industry. To attain organic certiﬁcation, operations ﬁrst submit an organic systems plan to the accredited certiﬁer of their choosing, and pay them a fee for certiﬁcation services. Once an organic systems plan has been reviewed and approved, the operation is inspected for congruence with the plan. These inspections are carried out on-site by an organic inspec- tor (which is the focus of this paper). NOP regulations (2016) specify that the person who makes a ﬁnal certiﬁcation decision (i.e. whether certiﬁcation is granted or not) cannot be the same individual who inspects an applicant operation. As a result, accredited certiﬁers either employ in-house inspectors, or contract with independent inspector personnel. Inspectors examine operation facilities, processes, and paperwork for congruence with the submitted or- ganic systems plan and accordance with NOP regulations. Discrepancies with the plan or potential regulatory violations are then reported to the accredited certiﬁer. According to NOP regulations, certiﬁcation must be renewed once a year, which entails submission of changes to the organic systems plan and an annual on-site inspection. The regulations require that certiﬁer personnel exhibit sufﬁcient expertise in organic production and handling to carry out certiﬁcation duties, but stop short of specifying what types of education, experience, or training constitute “sufﬁcient expertise” (NOP, 2016). As such, no formal requirements are speciﬁed for the education of organic inspectors. In practice, organic inspectors may attain training in two ways. First, inspectors that are hired as part-time D. P. Carter Regulatory inspector role perceptions 4 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 5: or full-time personnel from an accredited certiﬁer may receive in-house training (Kahn & Scoles 2013). Second, many inspectors receive training from the International Organic Inspectors Association (IOIA), a professional association with the express objective of providing quality organic inspector education (International Organic Inspectors Association [IOIA] n.d.). According to Margaret Scoles, IOIA Executive Director: “[the IOIA is] the industry standard. There is no rule that requires that anyone take our training, but almost all inspector trainees have taken a basic course with us” (Kahn & Scoles 2013). Reﬂecting the decentralized nature of NOP administration, no consolidated database exists on organic inspectors, and as a result, population characteristics are unknown. The IOIA lists 159 US inspector members; however, the IOIA is a voluntary professional organization representing an unspeciﬁed segment of the inspector population (IOIA n.d.). The composition of the study sample, and the manners in which inspectors were identiﬁed for the study, are provided in the remainder of this section. Research design This paper examines how organic inspectors under the NOP understand and approach their regulatory responsibilities – subjective experiences that are best understood by asking the inspectors themselves (Creswell 1994). To construct the study sample, organic inspectors were identiﬁed through a combination of targeted sampling based on organizational afﬁliations and snowball sampling techniques (Atkinson & Flint 2001). Inspectors were ﬁrst identiﬁed through the list of IOIA inspector members and Internet-based searches for inspectors identiﬁed on the websites of accredited certi- ﬁers. As the study progressed, and in particular during the interviews, participants veriﬁed as NOP regulation inspectors were asked to provide the contact information of colleagues. Certiﬁer administrators were also contacted, and requests were made for the contact information of current employee and contract inspector personnel. In total, the names and contact information of 260 individuals thought to be NOP regulation inspectors were collected; 159 were identiﬁed as IOIA members. The study followed a parallel mixed method research design in which qualitative and quantitative data were collected simultaneously (Leech & Onwuegbuzie 2007). The design merges inductive and deductive approaches (Van de Ven 2007). In doing so, it combines the strengths of qualitative data, such as nuanced research ﬁndings and the identiﬁcation of important considerations not included in a deductive framework (Gerring 2012), with the strengths of quantitative data: greater transparency and replicability, and data collection from a larger sample of partic- ipants (Creswell 1994; Teddlie & Tashakkori 2009). Quantitative data were collected through an online survey of potential organic inspectors. Qualitative data were collected through semi-structured interviews conducted with an inspector subsample. Organic inspector survey. An email request for participation in the online survey was sent to the 260 identiﬁed po- tential organic inspectors. In total, 107 responses were received, 10 of which indicated that the respondents no longer served as organic inspectors. Another ﬁve surveys were missing sufﬁcient data to render the results unusable, resulting in 92 serviceable surveys (35 percent of the initial 260 requests sent). The survey ﬁrst asked questions regarding respon- dents’ employment status, such as whether they were part-time, full-time, or contract employees. Respondents were also queried as to their organizational afﬁliations: the number of certiﬁers they worked for, and whether they worked for government or private (nonproﬁt or for-proﬁt) certiﬁers. The breakdown of respondents by work status and organizational afﬁliation is provided in Appendix I, alongside additional sample characteristics. The primary survey data were gathered in two question batteries. As the ﬁrst objective of this study was to under- stand how inspectors perceive their roles, a battery asked for respondents’ level of agreement with three role identiﬁ- cations: (i) regulatory monitor for the NOP, (ii) employee and representative of the certiﬁer(s) the inspector worked for, and (iii) a service provider to the operation under inspection. The second study objective was to ascertain inspectors’ attitudes toward discretion. A battery asked for respondents’ agreement with statements pertaining to three aspects of inspector discretion: (i) discretion in the interpretation of NOP regulations, (ii) discretion in reporting regulatee bookkeeping issues, and (iii) discretion in reporting potential noncompliance issues. Organic inspector interviews. Semi-structured interviews were conducted via telephone with a subsample of 10 organic inspectors. The subsample frame was purposeful and designed with the goal of achieving adequate representation of the Regulatory inspector role perceptions D. P. Because of a higher interview request response rate among government-afﬁliated inspectors, ﬁve interview participants were part-time or full-time employees of government certiﬁers, two were contract inspectors that worked for both government and private certiﬁers, and three were contract inspectors to exclusively private certiﬁers. Five of the 10 interviewees had been conducting organic inspections for over 10 years. One was a new organic inspector as of the preceding year, and the four remaining inspectors had conducted organic inspections for between four and six years. Four interviewees were women, and six were men. The interviewees were not asked for their exact age, however, two were over 65 years old, six were identiﬁed as “middle aged,” and the remaining two inspectors were under 30 years old. Interview questions focused on respondents’ experiences and perceptions of the inspector function in organic food regulation. A question speciﬁcally asked how respondents saw their role within the NOP context.3 The interviews were recorded, transcribed, and uploaded to QDA Miner software to facilitate qualitative analysis. A modiﬁed constant com- parison approach was applied to the interview data – transcripts were ﬁrst coded for discussion related to inspector role perceptions and the application of discretion (Glaser & Strauss 1967; Leech & Onwuegbuzie 2007). Coded data were then iteratively reviewed for themes within these general topics that emerged across the interviews (Creswell 1994; Leech & Onwuegbuzie 2007). The approach appropriately provided descriptive detail to quantitative survey ﬁndings and identiﬁed narrative explanations for study ﬁndings, as presented in the Discussion section of this paper (Gerring 2012). As a result of the overrepresentation of governmental employee inspectors in the interviewee sample, interviewees’ organizational afﬁliations and employment status were taken into consideration when qualitative data were considered for inclusion in this paper – in particular, in the case of direct quotations. Unique interviewee identiﬁers were assigned to each interview transcript, and reported in the paper to ensure that a minority of interviewee comments did not dispropor- tionately inﬂuence the ﬁndings.4 Results The ﬁrst research objective of this paper was to describe how inspectors perceive their regulatory roles, with the baseline expectation that in a decentralized regulatory context, such as the NOP, inspectors will perceive multiple, sometimes conﬂicting, role orientations. This expectation is examined in the ﬁrst part of this section. The second research objective – to examine the relationship between inspector role perceptions and attitudes toward discretion – follows. The interview ﬁndings corroborated the survey results. While all three role perceptions were expressed during the interviews, as exhibited in the quotes presented in Table 2, regulatory monitor was the most frequently cited role identiﬁcation, referenced in all 10 interviews. The comments of one interviewee seemed to summarize the average inspector’s role orientation succinctly, as “a kind of nested egg thing:” the inspector ﬁrst saw her role as a regulatory monitor.6 Within the regulatory monitor role, she was secondarily a certiﬁer’s employee, and within that role she provided a service to organic operations. Beyond the expectation that inspectors would perceive multiple role orientations, it was hypothesized that inspec- tors would at times experience conﬂict between their different roles. Interview respondents readily recognized role tensions that at times required them to negotiate competing expectations. In all of the examples provided by inter- viewees in which differing expectations were encountered, the responsibility that inspectors ascribed to their role as regulatory monitors appeared to supersede conﬂicting pressures. For example, one contract inspector interviewee described encountering a situation in which the inspector’s judgment of a noncompliance issue differed from that of their employing certiﬁer’s: This is what the certiﬁer said: ‘this is a dilemma between service and regulatory.’ I had a notion to tell him, well I think your ﬁrst job is regulatory service, I mean be polite and all that stuff and give them timeliness, but you are not going to give in and call it customer service. The results are displayed in Table 4. The ﬁrst hypothesis regarding this relationship predicted that the more an inspector aligns with a regulatory monitor role, the less likely he or she would be to hold favorable attitudes toward the use of discretion. Supporting the expectation, when respondents identiﬁed as NOP regulatory monitors, they took an unfavorable attitude toward the application of discretion. The support is limited, however, by correlation coefﬁcients that suggest relatively weak associations (r = 0.09 to 0.21), and only one statis- tically signiﬁcant ﬁnding. The second hypothesis anticipated that greater alignment with an organizational employee/representative role would be associated with a more favorable attitude toward the use of discretion. No support was found for the expectation, as the ﬁndings indicate virtually no relationship between inspectors’ alignment with the employee/representative role and their discretionary attitudes. In contrast, respondent identiﬁcation with a service provider role was positively associated with favorable discretionary attitudes across all three measures, supporting the ﬁnal hypothesis. Correlation coefﬁcients indicated weak-to-moderate relationships between the service provider role and discretionary attitudes (r = 0.18 to 0.38) that were statistically signiﬁcant at P < 0.10 to 0.01. Interview data shed further light on organic food inspectors’ attitudes toward discretion, and how these are related to inspectors’ understanding of their regulatory function. First, interview comments suggested that respondent oppo- sition to discretion is tied to the fact that NOP regulations mandate a clear distinction between inspectors as monitors and certiﬁer organizations as regulatory administrators. Several interviewees emphasized that it is the duty of inspectors to report on regulatees’ practices, and that judging the importance of potential noncompliance, or even determining whether an issue represents noncompliance, is the responsibility of certiﬁers. This sentiment was articulated well in the pronouncement: “As inspectors we are instructed to report ﬁndings rather than make decisions or express opinions.”12 Another interviewee stated: “As an inspector it is important to understand what our role is, and what our scope is – I don’t make certiﬁcation decisions.”13 Second, a NOP regulation conﬂict of interest provision stipulates certiﬁers (and by extension, inspectors) refrain from activities that might be considered “consultation” while administering regulations. Interviewees reported that as a result of this provision, they limited the ﬂexibility that they exhibited when interacting with regulatees, at times against their own inclinations or in manners that they felt were unproductive. For example, an employee inspector of a government certiﬁer compared inspecting for NOP regulations with administering other regulatory standards, stat- ing: “I always feel rather constrained, you know…in our other roles, we are allowed to help people, to problem solve, but we can’t do that as inspectors for the NOP.”14 Another interviewee lamented the “misfortune” that the conﬂict of interest provision represented for smaller organic operations. The ﬁndings suggest that a measured relationship exists between how inspectors perceive their roles and how they feel toward the use of inspector discretion: the more an inspector identiﬁes as a regulatory monitor, the more likely he or she is to favor a strict adherence to program rules. In contrast, the more an inspector identiﬁes as a regulatory service provider, the more likely he or she is to favor the use of discretion in a manner that might deviate from regulatory directives. The ﬁrst theoretical implication drawn from these ﬁndings is that the manners in which inspectors perceive their roles may be an overlooked factor in understanding inspection styles. Much of the existing literature assumes the nature of inspector-regulatee interactions is a strategic choice of inspectors seeking maximum compliance from regulated entities (e.g. May & Winter 2000; Pautz & Rinfret 2013). The quantitative and qualitative ﬁndings presented here indicate that inspectors’ attitudes toward the use of discretion, and, by extension, the rigidity or accommodation with which they approach their inspection duties, may also be related to how they prioritize their obligations and the individuals or entities that they are principally responsible to (Evans & Harris 2004). Despite the tension implied by discretionary attitudes that vary across different inspector role orientations, and qualitative data indicating that inspectors sometimes do experience such tensions, the results of this study suggest overall consistency in how American organic food inspectors understand and approach their functions. In a program context where regulatory administration is delegated to independent governmental and private sector organizations, and inspection duties are conducted by both in-house and contract employees, study ﬁndings depict role orientations that emphasize the regulatory aspect of inspectors’ responsibilities and general opposition to the use of inspector discretion. The second theoretical implication stemming from these ﬁndings is thus support for a growing body of empirical research that counters “conventional” predictions of street-level divergence from policy goals (Sandfort 2000; Brodkin 2012). Following the suggestion of an anonymous reviewer – as evidence continues to accumulate that inspectors and other street-level bureaucrats may act as “principled agents” (see also Brehm & Gates 1999) more often-than-not, it may be time to begin seriously questioning why, and through what mechanisms, such policy commitment is realized. To this end, qualitative study ﬁndings lend support to the theorized roles that several implementation context elements may contribute to consistency in regulatory program administration. First, aligning with social perspectives on the regulatory process (e.g. Fineman 1998; Nielsen 2006), Sandfort argues that front-line workers develop social schemas – “norms, collective beliefs, and shared knowledge” (2000, p. 742) – that transcend organizational and contextual differences, resulting in shared standards for street-level action. Consistency among organic inspector role perceptions could thus be the product of inspector norms established through formal mechanisms, such as the IOIA, and associated inspector interactions. As explained by Hill, nongovernmental implementation resources – such as professional associations – often carry the legitimacy and inﬂuence of peer authority ﬁgures, and thus “may constitute an unusually effective instrument for policy achievement” (2003, p. 280).
