Carter © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 9 Page 10: practices, through what one interviewee described as opportunities “to interact with peers to gain knowledge of on- the-ground variations in organic practice and discuss handling of real-world challenges.”16 A second factor that may help to explain the study ﬁndings is contingent on the extent to which organic inspectors assume regulatory monitor roles and oppose applications of inspector discretion because they condone and wish to support the NOP. Such an explanation maintains that consistency in frontline behavior may be attributable to inspector values and objectives that align with policy goals (Brehm & Gates 1999). This value-congruence explanation of study ﬁndings is supported by interview comments that highlight the importance of the consumer protection function of NOP regulations and identify inspectors as ultimately representing consumers.17 This explanation rests not only on value congruence, but also an alignment of interests. If the organic industry, including inspectors’ livelihoods, is dependent on public perceptions of regulatory integrity, then it is in the interest of inspectors to perform their duties in a manner that promotes consumer and citizen conﬁdence in the NOP. This is likely to be especially true if inspectors feel that their behavior is open to scrutiny – if the sentiment expressed by one interviewee is shared among his peers: “We work in a ﬁsh bowl.”18 A ﬁnal set of factors, reﬂecting a top-down approach to understanding policy implementation (Mazmanian & Sabatier 1983), suggests that formal institutional constraints imposed by the design of NOP regulations may have the intended effects of aligning inspector orientations with a regulatory monitor function and limiting the application of inspector discretion. Evidence of two inﬂuential regulatory design elements came through in the study ﬁndings. The ﬁrst is the distinction between inspectors as personnel responsible for monitoring certiﬁed operations and certiﬁers as the entities responsible for regulatory decisions, ofﬁcially leaving the application of judgment “up to the certiﬁer.”19 The second is the conﬂict of interest provision that prohibits inspectors from taking on an advisory role with regulated operations in the course of their regulatory duties. This provision may thus effectively inhibit inspectors from aligning too closely with regulatee concerns and interests to the extent that it undermines regulatory program ﬁdelity (Conglianese & Lazer 2003). Some caveats and study limitations This study leveraged the highly decentralized case of US organic food regulation to investigate inspector role percep- tions and discretionary attitudes. This case provided an appropriate context in which to examine how inspectors navigate the tensions stemming from conﬂicting obligations; however, it also calls for caveats regarding the generaliz- ability of the study ﬁndings. For example, the multiple roles incorporated in this study – particularly inspector- as-employee and inspector-as-service provider – may not be applicable in more traditional regulatory structures, where regulatees do not opt into regulatory oversight and where inspectors report directly to a regulatory agency. In addition, and as suggested above, several additional facets of the organic regulation context likely contributed to the results. With this in mind, the ﬁndings would be most appropriately compared to similarly decentralized regulatory contexts. Furthermore, this study’s results will prove most fruitful when the proposed explanations are tested in analogous contexts to better understand to what degree, and how, the identiﬁed factors contribute to program ﬁdelity. In addition to these caveats, the ﬁndings should be considered with an appreciation of three central limitations inherent in the study design and data. First, the fact that the population of inspectors that administer NOP regulations is undetermined makes it difﬁcult to assess the extent to which study participants adequately represent the US organic inspector body. To mitigate this limitation, established strategies for sampling unidentiﬁed populations were used, including targeted and snowball sampling (Atkinson & Flint 2001). Nonetheless, the effects of inadvertently omitting potential participants on study ﬁndings are ultimately unknown. Second, the study relied on data from a sample of 92 survey respondents and 10 interview participants. As with any research that depends on voluntary participation, study ﬁndings may be subject to some degree of nonresponse bias (Cresswell 1994). Third, and perhaps most importantly, the study relied on inspector self-reporting of role orientations and attitudes toward discretion. This approach, while arguably the most appropriate method for understanding inspectors’ subjec- tive experiences and perceptions (Creswell 1994), is particularly susceptible to social desirability bias in which study participants shape their responses to ﬁt the attributes and behaviors that they believe to be most socially acceptable (Podsakoff et al. 2003). In addition, asking respondents to identify their role orientations and discretionary attitudes in the abstract does not account for contextual variances; for example, if inspectors’ role orientations vary between regulatees perceived as “trustworthy” or “deceptive,” or if discretionary attitudes vary between routine situations and D. P. Carter Regulatory inspector role perceptions 10 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 11: exceptional circumstances or regulatory grey areas.
